title: Flag Emojis with adviz
text:
Get some country flags

import adviz
adviz.flag('pt'), adviz.flag('prt')

('🇵🇹', '🇵🇹')



adviz.flag('jp'), adviz.flag('jpn'), adviz.flag('JPN'), adviz.flag("JAPAN"), adviz.flag('japan')

('🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵')



adviz.flag('za')

'🇿🇦'

title: Flag Emojis with adviz
section: Use in an HTML string for more granular control
text:
Use in an HTML string for more granular control

html(f'<h3 style="font-size: 40pt; color: gold;">This is the flag of Zimbabwe: <span style="font-size: 60pt;">{adviz.flag("zw")}</span></h1>')
html(f'<h3 style="font-size: 40pt; color: steelblue;">This is the flag of Sweden: <span style="font-size: 60pt;">{adviz.flag("se")}</span></h1>')

This is the flag of Zimbabwe: 🇿🇼


This is the flag of Sweden: 🇸🇪

title: Flag Emojis with adviz
section: Adding country flags makes them easily recognizable
text:
Adding country flags makes them easily recognizable


Code
import plotly.express as px
fig = px.bar(
    population,
    x='country',
    y='population',
    height=500, 
    text=[adviz.flag(cc) for cc in population['code']],
    template='plotly_dark'
)
fig.data[0].textfont.size = 35
fig.data[0].marker.color = 'steelblue'
fig

title: Flag Emojis with adviz
text:
Horizontal chart


Code
fig = px.bar(
    population.sort_values('population'),
    y='country',
    x='population',
    orientation='h',
    height=500, 
    opacity=0.8,
    text=[adviz.flag(cc) for cc in population.sort_values('population')['code']],
    template='plotly_dark'
)
fig.data[0].textfont.size = 35
fig.data[0].marker.color = 'teal'
fig

python3 -m pip install adviz

title: Counting values
text:
Installation and usage
Installation:
python3 -m pip install adviz
Usage:
import adviz
adv.value_counts_plus([item_1, item_2, ... item_n], ...)

title: Counting values
text:
Usage
Generate a random list of 10,000 colors


Code
import random
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl

colors = list(mpl.colors.cnames.keys())
colors = random.choices(colors, weights=[0.9, 0.04, 0.05, 0.09]*37, k=10_000)
colors += [np.nan for i in range(240)]
colors[:20]


['mediumaquamarine',
 'linen',
 'mediumslateblue',
 'papayawhip',
 'palegoldenrod',
 'lightgreen',
 'rebeccapurple',
 'lightgreen',
 'darkred',
 'blanchedalmond',
 'cyan',
 'mediumslateblue',
 'mediumseagreen',
 'darkslategray',
 'dodgerblue',
 'linen',
 'lightsteelblue',
 'dodgerblue',
 'red',
 'lightcoral']

title: Counting values
Counting values
text:
Get started now:


python3 -m pip install adviz


Explore more advertools data visualizations

title: URL Structure
text: source

title: URL Structure
section: Read a list of URLs from a text/CSV file
text:
Read a list of URLs from a text/CSV file


Code
import advertools as adv
import adviz
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
import os
if os.getcwd().endswith('/nbs'):
    filepath = 'data/apple_url_list.csv'
else:
    filepath = 'nbs/data/apple_url_list.csv'

apple = pd.read_csv(filepath)
apple.head(10)









url




0
https://www.apple.com/ae/shop/accessories/all

https://www.apple.com/ae/shop/accessories/all/accessibility

4
https://www.apple.com/ae/shop/accessories/all/beats-featured

title: URL Structure
text:
Visualize the URL structure with

url_structure(apple['url'])

title: URL Structure
text:
Number of values per level

url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=5)

title: URL Structure
text:
Number of values per level

url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=25)

title: URL Structure
text:
Pick a theme

url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=25,
    theme='plotly_dark')

title: URL Structure
text:
Pick a theme

url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=15,
    theme='seaborn')

title: URL Structure
section: Set domain name and chart title
text:
Set domain name and chart title


Code
url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=15,
    theme='ggplot2',
    domain='apple.com',
    title='URL Structure: <b>apple.com</b><br>Raw data: <a href="data/apple_url_list.csv">Apple.com URLs</a>')

title: Status Codes
text: source

title: Status Codes
section: Change the theme with the theme parameter
text:
Change the theme with the theme parameter

status_codes(codes['status'], theme='seaborn')


                                                



status_codes(codes['status'], theme='plotly_dark')

title: Status Codes
section: Change the dimensions of the charts with width and height
text:
Change the dimensions of the charts with width and height

status_codes(codes['status'], width=700, height=700)

title: Status Codes
section:
Modify the title of the chart, and make use of various HTML tags , ,
, , ,
text:
Modify the title of the chart, and make use of various HTML tags <a>, <b>, <br>, <i>, <sub>, <sup>

website = 'MyWebsite.com'
title = f'Error Codes distribution <b>400</b> and <b>500</b><br>clickable: <i><a href="https://mywebsite.com">{website}</i> – April 3, 2023'

status_codes(
    codes.query('status >= 400')['status'],
    title=title,
    height=700,
    width=1300,
    theme='ggplot2')

title: Status Codes
section: Export the chart to an interactive, downloadable, emailable file with export_to_html
text:
Export the chart to an interactive, downloadable, emailable file with export_to_html

status_codes(
    codes['status'],
    title='Status Code - Exported to HTML',
    export_to_html='data/status_code_chart.html')

                                                



Try downloading, interacting with, and emailing this chart. File link

title: URL Structure
section: Read a list of URLs from a text/CSV file
text:
Read a list of URLs from a text/CSV file


Code
import advertools as adv
import adviz
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
import os
if os.getcwd().endswith('/nbs'):
    filepath = 'data/apple_url_list.csv'
else:
    filepath = 'nbs/data/apple_url_list.csv'
apple = pd.read_csv(filepath)
apple.head(10)

title: URL Structure
text:
Visualize the URL structure with

adviz.url_structure(apple['url'])

title: URL Structure
text:
Number of values per level

adviz.url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=5)

title: URL Structure
text:
Number of values per level

adviz.url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=25)

title: URL Structure
text:
Pick a theme

adviz.url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=25,
    theme='plotly_dark')

title: URL Structure
text:
Pick a theme

adviz.url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=15,
    theme='seaborn')

title: URL Structure
section: Set domain name and chart title
text:
Set domain name and chart title


Code
adviz.url_structure(
    url_list=apple['url'],
    items_per_level=15,
    theme='ggplot2',
    domain='apple.com',
    title='URL Structure: <b>apple.com</b><br>Raw data: <a href="data/apple_url_list.csv">Apple.com URLs</a>')

text:

python3 -m pip install adviz

title: Racing Bar Chart
text: source

title: Racing Bar Chart
text:
Entity-Metric-Period DataFrame


Code
# import random
# [random.randint(10, 35) for i in range(20)]

from IPython.display import display_html

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'entity': ['blue', 'green', 'yellow', 'red', 'orange'] * 4,
    'metric': [16, 35, 10, 25, 13, 35, 25, 25, 27, 19, 10, 18, 34, 20, 25, 20, 24, 25, 14, 21],
    'period': [i for i in range(1, 5) for x in range(5)],
}).sort_values(['period', 'metric'],
              ascending=[True, False]).reset_index(drop=True)
display_html(#'<div style="margin-left: 20%">' + 
             df
 .style
 .hide()
 .bar(subset=['metric'], color='lightgray')
 .background_gradient(subset=['period'], cmap='Blues')
 .to_html() 
             # + '</div>'
, raw=True)

title: Racing Bar Chart
section: Get the top three values for each period
text:
Get the top three values for each period

racing_chart(df, n=3)

title: Racing Bar Chart
text:
Important

The DataFrame supplied to the racing_chart function needs to have the three columns containing entity, metric, and period, in exactly this particular order. Their names don’t matter, but the order does


Entities
Some examples:

countries
URLs
keywords
product names



Metrics

clicks
impressions
sales
conversions
population
count



Period

days
months
weeks
quarters
years

title: Racing Bar Chart
section: Modifying the animation speed with animation_duration (in milliseconds)
text:
Modifying the animation speed with animation_duration (in milliseconds)

racing_chart(
    gsc[['country', 'clicks', 'date']],
    frame_duration=1500,
    height=700,
    title='Google Search Console <b>mywebsite.com</b><br>clicks per month - top 10<br><b>frame_duration=1500</b>',
    theme='plotly_dark')

title: Racing Bar Chart
section: Make it faster and use flags instead of country codes
text:
Make it faster and use flags instead of country codes

fig = racing_chart(
    gsc[['flag', 'clicks', 'date']],
    frame_duration=500,
    height=700,
    title='Google Search Console <b>mywebsite.com</b><br>clicks per month - top 10<br><b>frame_duration=500</b>',
    theme='plotly_dark')
fig.layout.yaxis.tickfont.size = 25
for frame in fig.frames:
    frame.data[0].marker.color = 'white'
fig.data[0].marker.color = 'white'
fig

title: Racing Bar Chart
section: Explore the top queries by month (impressions)
text:
Explore the top queries by month (impressions)
Queries can be long and take a lot of space, so we can set the left margin of the Figure object to a larger number to fit them.

fig = racing_chart(
    queries[['query', 'impressions', 'date']],
    title='Google Search Console <b>mywebsite.com</b><br>impressions per month - top 15',
    n=15,
    theme='seaborn',
    height=800)
fig.layout.margin.l = 250
fig

title: Racing Bar Chart
text:
Filtering entities
Taking a look at the top queries is definitely interesting, but many times you may have tens or hundreds of thousands of queries, and you want to go deeper.
One way is to filter those based on some criterion.
For example, let’s see which are the top queries that contain “log” and see how we are doing on log file analysis queries:

fig = racing_chart(
    queries[queries['query'].str.contains('log')][['query', 'impressions', 'date']],
    title='Google Search Console <b>mywebsite.com</b> - queries containing "log"<br>impressions per month - top 15',
    n=15,
    theme='seaborn',
    height=800)
fig.layout.margin.l = 250
fig

title: Flags
text:
Get some country flags

flag('pt'), flag('prt')

('🇵🇹', '🇵🇹')



flag('jp'), flag('jpn'), flag('JPN'), flag("JAPAN"), flag('japan')

('🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵', '🇯🇵')



flag('za')

'🇿🇦'

title: Flags
text:
Use in a Jupyter notebook
The following html and md functions enable HTML and markdown output to contain dynamic values.
from IPython.display import display_html as html, display_markdown as md
from functools import partial

html = partial(html, raw=True)
md = partial(md, raw=True)

title: Flags
text:
Use in a markdown string

md(f'# {flag("pr")}')

🇵🇷

title: Flags
section: Use in an HTML string for more granular control
text:
Use in an HTML string for more granular control

html(f'<h1 style="font-size: 40pt; color: steelblue;">This is the flag of Sweden: <span style="font-size: 60pt;">{flag("se")}</span></h1>')

This is the flag of Sweden: 🇸🇪

    'country': ['China', 'India', 'United States', 'Indonesia', 'Pakistan'],
    'population': [1439323776, 1380004385, 331002651, 273523615, 220892340],
    'code': ['cn', 'in', 'us', 'id', 'pk']
})

fig = px.bar(
    population,
    x='country',
    y='population',
    height=500, 
    text=[flag(cc) for cc in population['code']],
    template='ggplot2'
)
fig.data[0].textfont.size = 35
fig.data[0].marker.color = 'steelblue'
fig


                                                



fig = px.bar(
    population.sort_values('population'),
    y='country',
    x='population',
    orientation='h',
    height=500, 
    opacity=0.8,
    text=[flag(cc) for cc in population.sort_values('population')['code']],
    template='ggplot2'
)
fig.data[0].textfont.size = 35
fig.data[0].marker.color = 'teal'
fig
